flashcards.front: What is the primary purpose of a language model?
flashcards.back: To assign probabilities to word sequences to predict likelihood of text and generate coherent sentences.
A contiguous sequence of n items (words/characters) used for probabilistic text prediction.

flashcards.front: What is the Markov assumption in n-gram models?
flashcards.back: The probability of a word depends only on the previous n-1 words, not the entire history.
1. Predictive text input 2. Speech recognition 3. Spelling correction

flashcards.front: What is the main limitation of higher-order n-grams?
flashcards.back: Data sparsity - many possible combinations never appear in training data.
Measurement of how well a probability model predicts sample text (lower=better).
Technique adding small delta to all counts to handle unseen n-grams.

flashcards.front: What's the difference between bigram and trigram models?
flashcards.back: Bigram uses previous 1 word context, trigram uses previous 2 words.

flashcards.front: Why is backoff used in n-gram models?
flashcards.back: To handle missing n-grams by using lower-order models when higher-order counts are zero.

flashcards.front: What is the vocabulary size impact on n-gram models?
flashcards.back: Larger vocabularies increase model size and sparsity problems exponentially.

flashcards.front: How does interpolation differ from backoff?
flashcards.back: Interpolation mixes all n-gram levels, while backoff only uses lower when higher is zero.
Smoothing method using discounting and lower-order models for unseen n-grams.
Occurs when test data contains words not seen in training corpus.
Technique estimating probability of unseen events using frequency of seen events.

flashcards.front: Why are start/end tokens used in n-grams?
flashcards.back: To handle sentence boundaries and initial/final word predictions properly.
